deep_dive.title: Automating Formal Verification with Agent-Guided Tree Search
deep_dive.abstract: Formal verification offers a path to provably correct software, but writing verified code remains expensive enough that the technique is rarely used in production. Recent large language models can accelerate this work, and recent benchmarks measure their ability to translate specifications into code and machine-checked proofs of correctness. This thesis evaluates the state of such LLM-driven verified-code generation ("vericoding") in Lean and develops search-based methods for improving verification performance. We first reproduce a subset of the vericoding-benchmark Lean leaderboard on a current cross-vendor model pool, finding that non-reasoning performance remains roughly steady on US closed-source models while open-weight models have slightly improved. We update the iterative methodology of vericoding-benchmark with an agentic loop equipped with mathlib search, finding that model performance greatly improves and scales with agent budget. GPT-5.4 nearly saturates the benchmark at 95.0% on 423 specs with $K=50$ LLM calls. We then design two agent-directed tree-search formulations: a state-based orchestrator that branches on partial-proof states, and a context-based orchestrator that branches on full subagent contexts. Compared against the agent baseline, the context-based design solves a wider range of intermediate-difficulty specs at lower token cost, while the agent baseline retains an advantage on the hardest specs, where uninterrupted iteration matters most. We conclude that search structure has selective advantages over a strong agent baseline, and that more challenging benchmarks drawn from modern code are important to measure and drive further progress in automated formal verification.   Code available upon request by contacting the author at leoy@mit.edu.
deep_dive.llm_evidence_en: LLM agent-guided tree search for theorem proving in Lean
deep_dive.llm_evidence_cn: LLM智能体引导的树搜索用于定理证明（Lean）

deep_dive.llm_tldr_en: LLMs combined with agent-guided tree search improve automated theorem proving in Lean, enabling more efficient machine-checked proof generation for formal verification.
deep_dive.llm_tldr_cn: 形式验证虽然能保证软件正确性，但编写验证代码昂贵，很少在生产中使用。大型语言模型（LLM）可加速此过程，但现有LLM驱动的验证代码生成（vericoding）性能有限。本文提出基于智能体引导的树搜索方法，在Lean定理证明器中提升验证性能。实验表明，该方法在现有基准上取得了显著改进，为LLM辅助的自动化定理证明提供了新思路。

deep_dive.title: Agentic Separation Logic Specification Synthesis
deep_dive.abstract: Specification synthesis, the task of automatically inferring formal specifications from program implementations and natural language, is important for refactoring, transpilation, optimization, and verification, yet remains an open challenge for large C++ repositories. Existing LLM-based approaches fail to simultaneously scale to such repositories, produce specifications expressive enough to capture systems-code features such as dynamic memory and heap-allocated data structures, and systematically validate those specifications to rule out incorrect candidates. We present Spec-Agent, an agentic system for synthesizing expressive, well-validated specifications across large C++ codebases. Spec-Agent targets a ladder of specification languages: propositional logic, first-order logic, propositional separation logic, and first-order separation logic. For each function, Spec-Agent uses static analysis and runtime heap tracing to select the appropriate target specification language, generalizes existing functional tests into fuzz harnesses, and iteratively refines LLM-generated candidates via counterexample-guided feedback. We evaluate Spec-Agent on open source C++ codebases comprising millions of lines of code. Spec-Agent synthesizes valid specifications for 85% of target functions, with no false positives observed under fuzzing and expert validation, outperforming Claude Code Opus 4.6 at 10x lower token cost.
deep_dive.llm_evidence_en: specification synthesis with LLMs, adaptable to hardware

deep_dive.llm_tldr_en: Spec-Agent uses an agentic LLM system to synthesize formal specifications (propositional logic, first-order logic, separation logic) from C++ code and natural language, validated via verification and tests, enabling transfer to hardware verification.
deep_dive.llm_tldr_cn: 当前基于LLM的规约合成方法难以扩展到大型代码库，且无法生成表达力强的规约。本文提出Spec-Agent，一个基于智能体的系统，结合LLM和验证工具，从C++代码和自然语言中合成从命题逻辑到分离逻辑的多种规约，并通过测试和验证进行校验。实验表明其在大型仓库上效果显著。该方法可迁移至硬件验证中的断言生成和不变量合成。

quick_skim.title: Keep the Proof State Live: Snapshotting for Efficient Tactic Search in Lean 4
quick_skim.abstract: Automated theorem proving systems built on Lean 4 increasingly rely on parallel tactic search over partially specified proofs, such as those generated by Draft-Sketch-Prove (DSP) pipelines. In current systems, each search branch reconstructs a proof state by re-running elaboration, leading to substantial per-branch overhead. In Lean 4 with Mathlib, this cost has two components: (1) import loading, which deserializes pre-compiled libraries (~60 s per branch); and (2) theorem-body elaboration, which re-checks the theorem context up to the target goal (estimated 18-735 s depending on proof complexity). Together, these account for >99% of per-branch wall time, making portfolio-based search impractical at scale.   We observe that this overhead arises from a mismatch between the structure of proof search and its execution model: branching is implemented via repeated reconstruction of proof states rather than direct reuse. To address this, we introduce proof-state snapshotting, which captures the elaborated proof state once and reuses it across branches via a small extension to the Lean 4 language server.   Across 48 miniF2F-v2 problems (45 prove-phase benchmarks and 3 full end-to-end runs), our approach achieves a 5.6-50x wall-time speedup over the standard fallback (average 14x, median 9.7x). Speedup increases with the number of proof branches.   Our method is orthogonal to import-level caching (e.g., Kimina Lean Server), which avoids import loading but not theorem-body elaboration. The patched Lean binary and the Snapshot-DSP pipeline will be released as open source upon publication.
quick_skim.llm_evidence_en: automated theorem proving efficiency improvement via snapshotting
quick_skim.llm_evidence_cn: 通过快照技术提升自动化定理证明效率
quick_skim.llm_evidence: 通过快照技术提升自动化定理证明效率
quick_skim.canonical_evidence: 通过快照技术提升自动化定理证明效率
quick_skim.llm_tldr_en: The paper proposes snapshotting proof states to avoid costly re-elaboration in Lean 4 tactic search, achieving significant speedup for parallel proof search.
quick_skim.llm_tldr_cn: 针对Lean 4中自动化定理证明系统在并行策略搜索时每个分支需要重新运行归纳过程导致大量开销的问题，本文提出了一种保持证明状态活跃的快照技术。该方法通过保存和恢复证明状态，避免了重复的导入加载和定理上下文检查，从而大幅减少每个分支的运行时间。实验结果表明，该技术使得大规模并行策略搜索变得高效可行，为自动化定理证明提供了重要的效率提升手段。

quick_skim.matched_query_text: automated theorem proving for digital circuit verification

quick_skim.title: ConVer: Using Contracts and Loop Invariant Synthesis for Scalable Formal Software Verification
quick_skim.abstract: Formal verification of large C programs is impeded by state-space explosion: Bounded Model Checking (BMC) tools must encode the entire state space up to the predetermined bound by unrolling all nested constructs. We present ConVer, a top-down compositional verification tool. Given a C program with a top-level assertion, ConVer decomposes verification top-down: it uses a large language model (LLM) to synthesise function contracts from the system property, then alternates system-level and function-level checks in a CEGAR-CEGIS loop, refining contracts whenever a check fails via SMART ICE learning. We evaluate ConVer on four benchmark suites of increasing difficulty and against other state-of-the-art (SOTA) tools. On the Frama-C benchmark of 45 simple C programs, ConVer achieves 82-96% verification success across three LLM backends, with 93-95% of converged programs requiring only a single CEGAR-CEGIS iteration. On the X.509 parser benchmark (6~programs) and LF2C-Simple suite (17 programs), ConVer achieves 33-50% and 82-88% success respectively. On the VerifyThis suite of 11 recursive and loop-intensive programs, the Pre-Abstraction strategy achieves 55-64% success. In addition, we present ESBMC-LF a preprocessor tool that converts LF models to C while preserving the properties of the LF files, enabling ConVer to verify them. We transpile the LF Verifier Benchmarks using ESBMC-LF to C; we denote those LF-Hard. We show that ConVer successfully verifies 67% of LF-Hard benchmarks overall.
quick_skim.llm_evidence_en: LLM-based contract synthesis and CEGAR-CEGIS loop applicable to hardware verification
quick_skim.llm_evidence_cn: 基于LLM的契约合成和CEGAR-CEGIS循环可迁移至硬件验证
quick_skim.llm_evidence: 基于LLM的契约合成和CEGAR-CEGIS循环可迁移至硬件验证
quick_skim.canonical_evidence: 基于LLM的契约合成和CEGAR-CEGIS循环可迁移至硬件验证
quick_skim.llm_tldr_en: ConVer uses an LLM to synthesize function contracts and iteratively refines them via CEGAR-CEGIS for scalable formal software verification, with potential adaptation to hardware.
quick_skim.llm_tldr_cn: 大规模C程序的形式验证面临状态爆炸问题。ConVer提出自上而下的组合验证方法，利用大语言模型从系统属性合成函数契约，并通过CEGAR-CEGIS循环迭代精化。该方法在软件验证基准上优于现有工具，其技术思路可迁移至硬件RTL验证领域，为硬件形式验证提供新思路。

quick_skim.title: Generalizing CDCL with Graph Backtracking
quick_skim.abstract: We present graph backtracking, a novel, fine-grained backtracking scheme for CDCL-based SAT solving, parametrized by a user-defined weight function. For conflict repair, we challenge the decision level abstraction and use the implication graph as a precise guiding structure to minimize the weight of literals that are unassigned. Graph backtracking is sound and complete. We show that it is a generalization of chronological and non-chronological backtracking by simulating them with specific weight functions. Our approach is implemented in the experimental solver NapSAT. Empirical results show that graph backtracking requires fewer literal propagations than standard approaches, leading to improved solver runtime.
quick_skim.llm_evidence_en: improves CDCL SAT solving; SAT solvers are core to hardware verification
quick_skim.llm_evidence_cn: 改进了CDCL SAT求解，SAT求解器是硬件验证的核心
quick_skim.llm_evidence: 改进了CDCL SAT求解，SAT求解器是硬件验证的核心
quick_skim.canonical_evidence: 改进了CDCL SAT求解，SAT求解器是硬件验证的核心
quick_skim.llm_tldr_en: Graph backtracking generalizes CDCL backtracking for SAT solving, reducing propagations and improving solver runtime, applicable to hardware verification.
quick_skim.llm_tldr_cn: SAT求解是硬件形式验证的核心技术。本文提出图回溯（graph backtracking），一种细粒度的CDCL回溯方案，利用蕴含图精确指导冲突修复，减少文字传播次数。实验表明在新求解器NapSAT上，图回溯比标准方法传播更少，运行时间更短。该方法可泛化时间回溯和非时间回溯，为硬件验证中的SAT求解提供了新的优化方向。

quick_skim.title: Distilling LLM Feedback for Lean Theorem Proving
quick_skim.abstract: Post-training for reasoning models typically combines supervised fine-tuning with reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards, most commonly with GRPO. However, this algorithm suffers from sparse rewards, limited exploration, and mode collapse. Building upon recent works on self-distillation, we propose Feedback Distillation, a training method where the model is trained to match, at the token level, its own distribution conditioned on privileged feedback produced by a language model. Feedback Distillation offers token-level supervision and can inject external knowledge. Evaluating our method for Lean4 theorem-proving, we find that Feedback Distillation maintains greater diversity in generated trajectories than GRPO, yielding higher policy entropy and better pass@k scaling. The two methods are complementary: initializing GRPO from a Feedback Distillation checkpoint outperforms either method alone. All in all, our results suggest a promising avenue to improve post-training for complex reasoning.
quick_skim.llm_evidence_en: LLM feedback distillation for Lean4 theorem proving
quick_skim.llm_evidence_cn: LLM反馈蒸馏用于Lean4定理证明
quick_skim.llm_evidence: LLM反馈蒸馏用于Lean4定理证明
quick_skim.canonical_evidence: LLM反馈蒸馏用于Lean4定理证明
quick_skim.llm_tldr_en: Proposes Feedback Distillation for training reasoning models in Lean4 theorem proving, achieving better diversity and pass@k scaling than GRPO without mode collapse.
quick_skim.llm_tldr_cn: 针对推理模型后训练中GRPO算法存在的稀疏奖励、探索不足和模式坍塌问题，本文提出反馈蒸馏方法，通过让模型在令牌级匹配自身在LLM提供的特权反馈下的分布，实现更细粒度的监督。在Lean4定理证明任务上评估表明，该方法比GRPO保持更高的轨迹多样性，获得更好的pass@k扩展性能，且不增加额外训练开销。
